intent: give me a nicer way to browse my clipboard history on mac

suggestions.title: Install Maccy — lightweight open-source clipboard manager for macOS
suggestions.rationale: Maccy is a tiny, open-source, keyboard-driven clipboard history app tailored to macOS. Installable via Homebrew Cask, lives in the menu bar, global hotkey (default Cmd+Shift+C) pops a fuzzy-searchable list of recent clips. Default pick for a 'nicer clipboard history' with no fuss — fits this repo's pattern of a new tools/<name>/ dir with a cask install.

suggestions.title: Install Raycast (includes best-in-class Clipboard History)
suggestions.rationale: Raycast's Clipboard History command is arguably the best on macOS — supports text, images, and files with rich previews, pinning, and fuzzy search. If you're open to replacing Spotlight/Alfred too, this is two-for-one. Homebrew Cask install. Heavier than Maccy but far more capable and extensible.

suggestions.title: Install CopyQ — cross-platform, scriptable clipboard manager
suggestions.rationale: CopyQ is GPL and cross-platform, so one playbook could cover macOS plus your debian/arch hosts. Supports tabs, tags, rich content, and a scripting engine for clipboard transforms. UI is dated but the most powerful of the options.

suggestions.title: Add an fzf-based clipboard history picker wired into zsh
suggestions.rationale: You already have fzf installed (tools/fzf/). A small background pbpaste poller maintains a ring of recent clips in a file, and a zsh widget bound to e.g. Ctrl+Y fuzzy-picks one back onto the clipboard via pbcopy. Terminal-native, no GUI dependency, plays to the fzf-first workflow you already use. Only covers terminal usage, not GUI apps.

suggestions.title: Add TPM + tmux-yank (+ extrakto) for tmux-native clipboard & buffer picking
suggestions.rationale: Your tmux config has no plugin manager or yank integration yet. tmux-yank syncs tmux copy-mode selections with the macOS system clipboard via pbcopy. extrakto adds a fuzzy picker over visible pane output, which in practice acts like 'browse things I might want to copy'. Complements (doesn't replace) a system-wide manager.

suggestions.title: Use 1Password Quick Access (Cmd+Shift+Space) for credential pastes
suggestions.rationale: 1Password is already in tools/1password/. Its Quick Access popover (Cmd+Shift+Space) lets you search and copy credentials/notes with one shortcut, and it auto-clears the clipboard. Not a general clipboard history, but if most of what you paste is secrets, this may solve 70% of the pain with zero new tools — a settings tweak only.

suggestions.title: Install Clipy — free, minimal clipboard history with snippets
suggestions.rationale: Clipy is a free, open-source, long-running fork of ClipMenu. Menu bar icon, configurable hotkey, and — unlike Maccy — a built-in snippet manager for reusable templates. Good middle ground if you paste the same phrases often.
